title: Soft honey nougat
subtitle: Chewy honey-scented nougat with toasted almonds and pistachios
excerpt: Twenty-four pieces of soft nougat: honey cooked to 121°C and poured into whipped egg whites, followed by a caster sugar and glucose syrup cooked to 145°C, beaten until chewy and white, then folded through with warm toasted almonds and pistachios and pressed into a lined tin.
sourceNotes: Soft nougat (nougat blanc or nougat de Montélimar) is documented in French and Italian confectionery from the nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903): the nougat section documents the two-syrup method : honey is cooked to soft ball stage and poured into egg whites, followed by a firmer sugar syrup as the technique for producing a soft, chewy nougat with a stable aerated structure. The addition of toasted almonds and pistachios is documented in both French and Italian public-domain confectionery literature of the period. The recipe here follows the two-syrup public-domain method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Nougat keeps in an airtight tin, wrapped in baking paper, for 2 weeks. The texture improves after 24 hours.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target: 145°C for the main sugar syrup; honey syrup cooked separately to 121°C first — use a sugar thermometer for both

recipeTools.notes: Both syrups require hands-free pouring — a hand mixer is not safe here.
Two saucepans: one for the honey, one for the sugar syrup.
20 × 20 cm tin lined with rice paper or greased and lined with baking paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: Adding toasted nuts while they are still warm (not cold from a rack) when folding them into nougat or candy. Cold nuts lower the temperature of the nougat too quickly, causing it to stiffen before the nuts are evenly distributed. Warm nuts are pliable to fold and do not shock the mixture into premature setting. Toast the nuts in the oven while the syrups are cooking and fold them in straight from the tray.

content.text: Nougat requires two separate syrups poured in sequence into whipped egg whites. The honey syrup goes in first at 121°C, which sets the initial aeration. The hotter sugar syrup at 145°C follows, which firms the structure. The result is chewy rather than hard, and the honey perfumes the whole batch. Add the nuts warm, straight from the oven —
in without cooling the nougat prematurely.

attrs.title: Hot sugar is extremely dangerous
attrs.body: Both syrups reach between 121°C and 145°C and cause severe burns on contact with skin. Pour both syrups in a thin stream down the inside wall of the mixer bowl — never directly onto the whisk. Keep children away. Run any skin contact immediately under cold water for at least 10 minutes.

items.prepNote: stabilises the egg white while the syrups are prepared
a strongly flavoured honey — acacia or heather
toasted in the oven at 180°C for 8 minutes, kept warm

content.text: Toast the nuts and prepare the tin

Toast the {{blanched-almonds}} almonds and {{pistachios}} on a baking tray at 180°C fan for 8 minutes until lightly golden. Keep in the oven (turned off) to stay warm. Line a 20 × 20 cm tin with rice paper or greased baking paper. Have both tins and all ingredients measured and ready before starting the syrups — nougat waits for no one.
Whip egg whites and add the honey syrup

Begin whipping the {{egg-whites}} egg whites in a stand mixer on high speed. When they reach soft peaks, add the {{caster-sugar}} 50 g of caster sugar and whip to firm peaks. Simultaneously, heat the {{honey}} in a small saucepan over medium heat to 121°C. Pour the hot honey in a thin stream down the inside of the bowl while the mixer continues on high. The mixture will increase in volume and turn slightly golden.

Combine the {{caster-sugar}} remaining caster sugar, {{glucose-syrup}}, and {{water}} in a medium saucepan and cook to 145°C without stirring once boiling. Pour this syrup in a thin stream into the still-beating egg white and honey mixture. Add {{vanilla-extract}}. Beat on high speed for 5–8 minutes until the nougat is very thick, opaque, white, and holds its shape when the mixer is stopped.

Remove the bowl from the mixer. Add the warm nuts and fold through quickly with a spatula until evenly distributed. Scrape into the prepared tin immediately. Press flat with wet hands or a piece of rice paper placed on top. Leave to cool at room temperature for at least 4 hours, or overnight. Cut into 24 pieces with a sharp knife dipped in hot water between cuts.
